script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg and this is The Portfolio Podcast. Tuesday, as of June 30 2026 at 4:45 AM ET — here is your Q-Q-Q-M recap. The portfolio closed at one hundred two thousand eight hundred forty-nine dollars and fifty-eight cents, up two thousand eight hundred forty-nine dollars and fifty-eight cents on the session. That is a gain of two point eight five percent. A strong day across the board.

Tesla, T-S-L-A, surged nearly ten percent in a single session. That is not a rounding error. That is a near double-digit move on one of the most widely watched names in the entire market — and it came with no headline catalyst in the news feed as of this recording. When a stock moves that hard, that fast, without an obvious trigger, it demands scrutiny.

Turning to the broad market. Tuesday's session showed clear momentum in large-cap technology and consumer discretionary names, with several Magnificent Seven components posting significant gains. Microsoft, M-S-F-T, added four point four six percent, Amazon, A-M-Z-N, climbed five point seven eight percent, and Meta, M-E-T-A, rose three point six three percent, suggesting broad risk appetite was elevated. No major macro print dominated the tape as of this recording, though a recent Federal Reserve inflation forecast — noted in circulating market commentary — contains what analysts are describing as an unpleasant surprise for Wall Street, with crude oil below seventy dollars per barrel not telling the full inflation story. Watch that thread closely.

The top story of Tuesday's session belongs to Microsoft, M-S-F-T. The stock closed at three hundred sixty-eight dollars and fifty-seven cents, up fifteen dollars and seventy-four cents, a gain of four point four six percent. That is one of the strongest single-session moves for the name in recent memory, and it came alongside two meaningful developments. First, Microsoft announced it has become a licensee of the Sisvel Wi-Fi Multimode patent pool, joining a roster that includes A-S-U-S, Hewlett Packard Enterprise, Sony, Huawei, Panasonic, and Philips, among others. The announcement was made out of Luxembourg and published at four seventeen AM Eastern time. Patent licensing moves of this kind signal proactive intellectual property management — important as Wi-Fi connectivity becomes embedded in AI infrastructure. Second, and arguably more consequential for the longer-term thesis, Tech Mahindra announced a collaboration with Microsoft to showcase a Network Digital Twin solution targeting communications service providers. The goal is to help carriers modernize network operations, improve service performance, and accelerate monetization of next-generation five-G capabilities. For M-S-F-T holders inside Q-Q-Q-M — where the stock carries an eight point four two percent portfolio weight — this is exactly the kind of enterprise AI infrastructure story that underpins the bull case. Azure growth, AI tooling, and now telecom modernization. The session's move suggests the market is pricing in continued execution.

Apple, A-A-P-L, closed at two hundred eighty-one dollars and seventy-four cents, up six dollars and fifty-nine cents, a gain of two point four zero percent. A-A-P-L carries the largest weight in the portfolio at eight point nine seven percent, so this move contributed meaningfully to Tuesday's overall gain. The news picture is mixed. Britain's Competition and Markets Authority proposed on Tuesday allowing app developers to direct users to alternative payment options outside Apple and Google's app stores. The proposal targets fees and competition dynamics in the UK developer ecosystem. For Apple specifically, this represents a continued global regulatory squeeze on the App Store model — a revenue stream that has become increasingly central to the services business. Separately, and more concerning, Apple has reportedly asked the US government for approval to purchase memory chips from blacklisted Chinese supplier ChangXin Memory Technologies amid an AI-driven chip shortage. A ransomware attack on Indian supplier Tata Electronics has also resulted in a leak of sensitive iPhone eighteen Pro component and manufacturing data. Supply chain resilience and regulatory exposure are both in the spotlight ahead of future hardware launches. The stock moved higher despite these headwinds, which suggests the market is discounting near-term risk — but these are threads worth monitoring closely.

Microsoft, M-S-F-T, was covered in the top story. Eight point four two percent of the portfolio, up four point four six percent. The enterprise AI and telecom infrastructure narrative is intact.

Nvidia, N-V-D-A, closed at one hundred ninety-four dollars and ninety-seven cents, down seventy-seven cents, a loss of zero point three nine percent. Against a broadly bullish tape, this mild underperformance stands out. N-V-D-A carries eight point one five percent of the portfolio. The news items tagged to Nvidia on Tuesday are largely tangential — stories on memory chip stocks Micron and Sandisk, cryptocurrency comparisons, and pharmaceutical commentary. None of it is directly material to N-V-D-A. What is worth noting is the broader AI memory stock narrative: Micron and Sandisk are reportedly up two hundred percent in the last three months, with historical precedent suggesting mean reversion risk. That dynamic touches the AI chip ecosystem broadly. N-V-D-A's flat session amid broad market strength is a data point, not a conclusion — but relative underperformance on a green tape is worth flagging.

Alphabet, G-O-O-G-L, closed at three hundred fifty-three dollars and sixty-five cents, up nine dollars and ninety-four cents, a gain of two point eight nine percent. At eight point zero one percent of the portfolio, this was a constructive session. G-O-O-G-L joined the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon — a symbolic but meaningful index inclusion that broadens the institutional buyer base. Year to date, the stock is up twelve point two percent, and over the past twelve months it has delivered one hundred one point seven percent. Google is also renewing its campaign to relax AI copyright laws in the UK, arguing that failure to act will harm the economy. Regulatory and legislative outcomes on AI intellectual property remain a key variable for the advertising and cloud businesses alike. The bullish sentiment score on G-O-O-G-L news this session registered at zero point four six — cautiously constructive.

Amazon, A-M-Z-N, closed at two hundred forty dollars and fourteen cents, up thirteen dollars and thirteen cents, a gain of five point seven eight percent. At five point five two percent of the portfolio, this was a strong contributor to Tuesday's gains. No specific news was flagged for A-M-Z-N in Tuesday's feed, but the magnitude of the move — nearly six percent — in the context of broad tech strength suggests institutional rotation into consumer discretionary and cloud-facing names. AWS and advertising momentum remain the core catalysts to watch into the back half of twenty twenty-six.

Broadcom, A-V-G-O, closed at three hundred seventy-two dollars and forty-five cents, down six dollars and forty-six cents, a loss of one point seven zero percent. At four point six one percent of the portfolio, this was the second-weakest session performer after N-V-D-A. No specific news was tagged to A-V-G-O on Tuesday. The pullback comes in a session where semiconductor names were broadly mixed — N-V-D-A slightly down, A-V-G-O more notably lower. Custom AI silicon and networking chip demand remain the longer-term thesis, but Tuesday's session was a quiet reminder that semis can diverge sharply within a single tape.

Meta Platforms, M-E-T-A, closed at five hundred sixty-two dollars and sixty cents, up nineteen dollars and seventy-three cents, a gain of three point six three percent. At four point four eight percent of the portfolio, Meta had a clean session. No specific news was attached to M-E-T-A in Tuesday's feed. The move tracks with broader communication services strength on the day. Ad revenue resilience and continued AI infrastructure investment — including the Llama model ecosystem — remain the core narratives heading into the second half of the year.

Tesla, T-S-L-A, closed at four hundred eleven dollars and eighty-four cents, up thirty-six dollars and seventy-two cents, a gain of nine point seven nine percent. At three point five four percent of the portfolio, this was the single largest percentage mover in the entire holdings list on Tuesday. No specific catalyst was identified in the news feed as of four forty-five AM Eastern. A nearly ten percent single-session move without a confirmed headline is unusual. Watch for any after-hours filing, product announcement, or analyst note that may surface as the morning progresses.

Costco, C-O-S-T, closed at nine hundred forty-six dollars and sixty-eight cents, up four dollars and forty-four cents, a gain of zero point four seven percent. At two point eight one percent of the portfolio, this was the quietest session in the holdings lineup. No news was flagged. Consumer staples broadly lagged the tape on Tuesday, which is consistent with a risk-on rotation. C-O-S-T remains a steady compounder — the story here is membership renewal rates and same-store sales, with the next earnings report the primary near-term catalyst.

Three numbers to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, the Federal Reserve's June inflation forecast details — referenced in market commentary as containing a surprise divergence from crude oil pricing trends — could move rate-sensitive tech valuations if the full data hits wires Wednesday. Second, any official US government response to Apple's request for approval to source chips from ChangXin Memory Technologies will directly affect A-A-P-L's supply chain narrative and could move the stock. Third, watch for any filing or announcement tied to Tesla's unexplained nine point seven nine percent surge — a move of that magnitude typically precedes or follows a disclosure.

That is your Q-Q-Q-M recap for Tuesday, June 30 2026. The next episode drops at five PM Eastern on the next trading day. Keep your eye on the Tesla story — when a name moves ten percent without a headline, the headline is coming. I am Josh Weinberg. This has been The Portfolio Podcast.
